summary: Fresh adversarial perf audit of the Content Engine. The prior QA pass (c09439b) landed real wins: single-pass card partitioning, memoized ContentCard, lazy images, presence interval pausing. Remaining hot spots are concentrated in pipeline-context.tsx: logAudit fires on every moveCard before DB confirmation (phantom write + extra RPC even on rollback), the localStorage backup effect re-stringifies the full board on every single-card mutation, and the realtime DELETE handler is identity-stable but UPDATE/INSERT echoes rebuild whole arrays. The drive stream route fetches a Google access token twice per request. No iron-law guard is weakened by any recommended fix.

findings.title: logAudit() fires on every moveCard before DB confirmation, including failed and temp-id moves
findings.description: moveCard() calls logAudit(cardId, ...) unconditionally at line 549, after the optimistic setCards but outside the async DB block. logAudit issues a record_audit_event RPC round-trip. This runs even when (a) the card is a temp timestamp id with no DB row, (b) useSupabase is false, and (c) most importantly when the supabase.from('posts').update() inside the async IIFE later FAILS and the move is rolled back. The result is a phantom 'stage_change' audit row for a move that never happened, plus one extra Supabase RPC per drag regardless of outcome. Compare submitReapproval / submitKickback, which were already fixed to fire logAudit only inside the success branch (lines 598, 699) to close DATA-002. moveCard was missed by that fix.
findings.reproducer: Drag any card to a new column while RLS or a DB trigger rejects the update (e.g. move a card the user lacks workspace membership for). The card snaps back via rollback() but audit_log_v2 still gains a stage_change entry. Also: every successful move = 1 update + 1 separate record_audit_event RPC = 2 round-trips.
findings.recommendedFix: Move the logAudit(cardId, currentUser.name, 'stage_change', ...) call from line 549 into the async IIFE, immediately after 'stageUpdated = true;' (line 487), so it only fires once the DB UPDATE commits. For the local-only / non-Supabase branch, keep an else path that logs locally, mirroring submitKickback's fireNotifications pattern. Does not touch any iron-law guard.

findings.title: localStorage backup effect re-serializes the entire board on every single-card mutation
findings.description: The persist effect runs saveState(STORAGE_KEY, cards) with [cards] as its dependency. saveState does a synchronous JSON.stringify of the full card list (ContentCard is a wide object with checklist, revisionHistory, carouselOutline, sourceNotes, asset arrays, etc). Every updateCard keystroke in the drawer, every moveCard, every realtime UPDATE/INSERT echo, and every optimistic mutation produces a new cards array and triggers a full synchronous stringify on the main thread. With a realistic board the stringified payload is tens to hundreds of KB; the team-context already debounces the equivalent write (PERF-015, 500ms) but pipeline-context was not given the same treatment. Editing a caption in the drawer fires a stringify on each character.
findings.reproducer: Open the asset drawer, type into the caption field — InlineEdit/updateCard updates cards on each change, the [cards] effect fires JSON.stringify(fullBoard) synchronously per keystroke. Also fires on every realtime echo from other users.
findings.recommendedFix: Apply the same debounce team-context.tsx uses (PERF-015): wrap the saveState in a window.setTimeout (e.g. 500-1000ms) keyed by a ref, clearing the prior timer on each cards change, and flush on unmount. localStorage is only a fallback-on-DB-error backup so a sub-second write delay is harmless and does not affect the iron-law load() path.

findings.title: Drive stream route fetches a Google access token twice per request, sequentially
findings.description: GET() calls getFileMetadata(fileId) on line 104, which internally calls driveFetch -> getAccessToken (google-drive.ts:45). Line 105 then calls getAccessToken() again directly. getAccessToken() invokes GoogleAuth.getClient().getAccessToken() each time. The google-auth-library does cache tokens internally so the second call is usually cheap, but the two awaits are strictly sequential and the metadata fetch + token fetch could overlap. Every /
findings.reproducer: Load the Media Library or any board with Drive-hosted thumbnails. Each thumbnail hits /api/drive/stream, which awaits getFileMetadata then awaits getAccessToken sequentially before the Drive fetch even starts.
findings.recommendedFix: Run the two independent awaits concurrently: const [meta, token] = await Promise.all([getFileMetadata(fileId), getAccessToken()]). Removes one round-trip of serial latency from every streamed asset request. Pure parallelization, no behavior change.

findings.title: auth-context onAuthStateChange triggers two whole-tree re-render cascades per non-refresh event
findings.description: On any onAuthStateChange event other than TOKEN_REFRESHED (SIGNED_IN, USER_UPDATED, etc), the handler calls setCurrentUser(buildProfile(...)) synchronously, then enrichFromTeamMembers(...).then((enriched) => setCurrentUser(enriched)). That is two separate currentUser identity changes. currentUser is in the AuthContext value useMemo dependency list, so each change rebuilds the auth context value and cascades re-renders through every consumer: PipelineProvider, TeamProvider, PresenceProvider, AppShell, TopBar, Sidebar, every page. The prior pass correctly fixed the TOKEN_REFRESHED case (PERF-010) but left the SIGNED_IN path doing a double-set. buildProfile already produces a usable profile; the enrich pass only adds avatar/role nuance.
findings.reproducer: Sign in, or trigger any USER_UPDATED auth event. The whole provider tree re-renders twice in quick succession before settling.
findings.recommendedFix: Skip the first setCurrentUser when the email is unchanged from the existing currentUser (re-enrich into the same identity), or gate the enrich result with a shallow equality check before calling setCurrentUser the second time — only set if name/role/avatar actually differ. Low risk; identical to the equality guard already used in AvatarSync (app-shell.tsx:285).

findings.title: Studio polling interval is torn down and recreated on every rows state change
findings.description: The job-status polling effect depends on [rows, jobIdByRow, addToast]. rows changes on every realtime echo, every debounced field save, and every poll-driven setRows. Each change clears the 3s interval and starts a fresh one, so the polling cadence is effectively reset constantly while the user edits the plan grid — the timer rarely survives a full 3s window during active editing, which both wastes the just-scheduled callback and can delay the actual job-status check. The effect also closes over the 'generating' snapshot captured at effect-creation time; combined with frequent recreation this is churny rather than incorrect.
findings.reproducer: Open Creator Studio with a generating row, then type into any field. Each keystroke -> onFieldChange -> setRows -> effect cleanup+recreate -> interval restarts. The 3s poll keeps getting reset.
findings.recommendedFix: Split the concern: keep a stable interval (depend only on a boolean 'hasActiveJobs') and read the current rows/jobIdByRow from refs inside the tick, or move the generating-row derivation into the tick body. This keeps one steady 3s timer regardless of unrelated rows edits.

findings.title: PipelineColumn subscribes to full pipeline context only to read isLoading, re-rendering all 5 columns on every card mutation
findings.description: PipelineColumn calls usePipeline() and destructures only isLoading. usePipeline returns the single large context value object that is rebuilt (new identity) whenever cards, selectedCard, isDrawerOpen, or any other context field changes — which is on every optimistic mutation and every realtime echo. Because PipelineColumn is not memoized and consumes the context, all 5 columns re-render on every card change even though only the affected column's `cards` prop actually changed and isLoading is stable after first load. The cards prop itself is a fresh array each render (kanban-board's sortedColumnCards rebuilds the groups object), so React.memo on the column would not fully help without also stabilizing per-column arrays — but dropping the context subscription removes one of the two re-render triggers.
findings.reproducer: With the board open, move or edit any single card. React DevTools Profiler shows all 5 PipelineColumn instances re-rendering, not just the source and destination columns.
findings.recommendedFix: Pass isLoading down as a prop from KanbanBoard (which can read it once) instead of having each column subscribe to the whole pipeline context. KanbanBoard already calls usePipeline. This removes the context-value-identity re-render trigger from the column. Sorting/memoizing the per-column arrays is a larger follow-up but the prop change is minimal and safe.

findings.title: moveCard / updateCard / submitReapproval / submitKickback / deleteCard all depend on full cards array, recreating every callback on every mutation
findings.description: moveCard (dep [useSupabase, cards, currentUser.name, addToast]), updateCard, submitReapproval, submitKickback, requestReapproval, requestKickback, and deleteCard all list `cards` in their useCallback dependency arrays so they can do cards.find(c => c.id === cardId). Because cards changes on every mutation, every one of these callbacks gets a new identity on every render, which makes the context `value` useMemo (line 813) recompute and propagate a new context object to all 16 usePipeline consumers. The find() only needs the current snapshot — it does not need to be a dependency if read from a ref.
findings.reproducer: Any card mutation invalidates every action callback identity, defeating downstream memoization in consumers that receive these callbacks as props/deps.
findings.recommendedFix: Keep a cardsRef (useRef) synced to cards via a tiny effect, and have the action callbacks read cardsRef.current instead of closing over cards. Then drop `cards` from their dependency arrays. The callbacks become stable, the context value stops churning on every mutation. Verify the isValidUuid and workspace_id guards are untouched — they are independent of this change.

findings.title: POSTS_SELECT_FULL uses a two-level nested join that is fetched then discarded until migrations 0010-0014 ship
findings.description: POSTS_SELECT_FULL selects '*, publish_jobs(state, platform_publish_attempts(platform, state, external_post_id))'. AGENTS.md section 7 states migrations 0010-0014 are NOT applied to production, so this select currently always errors on the first try and the code falls back to POSTS_SELECT_BASIC. That means the initial board load reliably pays TWO Supabase round-trips (failed FULL, then BASIC) on every app load until those migrations land. Functionally correct (the fallback is mandated and must stay) but it is a guaranteed wasted request on the hottest path right now.
findings.reproducer: Every app load / pipeline-context load(): supabase.from('posts').select(POSTS_SELECT_FULL) errors -> second call with POSTS_SELECT_BASIC. Two network round-trips before the board renders.
findings.recommendedFix: Do not remove the fallback. Optionally gate the FULL attempt behind an env flag (e.g. NEXT_PUBLIC_PUBLISH_LEDGER_READY) so production skips the known-failing FULL select and goes straight to BASIC, eliminating the wasted round-trip until 0010-0014 are applied. Flip the flag when migrations ship. If a flag is unwanted, leave as-is and treat this as informational.

findings.title: dbToCard runs two matchAll regex scans over the notes field on every row map and every realtime echo
findings.description: dbToCard reconstructs revised flag and revisionHistory by running one test() and two matchAll() regexes over row.notes. This runs once per row during the initial load().map(dbToCard) (fine, one-time) but ALSO runs on every realtime INSERT and UPDATE echo (lines 384, 397). For a workspace with active collaborators, every peer edit re-parses the full notes blob of the changed post with two global-flag regex passes. Notes can grow large as revision history accumulates inline. Minor per-event cost but it is on the realtime path which fires for every peer mutation.
findings.reproducer: While another user edits posts, each realtime UPDATE payload triggers dbToCard -> 3 regex passes over that post's notes string.
findings.recommendedFix: Low priority. If revisionHistory and revised are ever persisted as real columns, read them directly instead of regex-reconstructing. Until then, the cost is acceptable; flag only as informational. No change required for this deploy.
